body.summary: We consider this governance action to be constitutional.
body.rationaleStatement: This proposal is a Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action to withdraw ₳7,920,000 from the Cardano Treasury for Input Output’s “Blockfrost: Maintenance and Next Generation Indexing.” In accordance with Article 2, Section 6, Paragraphs 1 and 2 of the Cardano Constitution, this proposal adopts an immutable off-chain reference using IPFS. Furthermore, the proposal text includes the title, summary, motivation, deliverables, roadmap, budget allocation, KPIs, risks, and audit and oversight mechanisms; we therefore determine that it presents information sufficient for evaluation as a Governance Action. Article 2, Section 7, Paragraphs 1 and 2 outline the purpose, implementation period, cost breakdown, and repayment conditions of the Treasury Withdrawal. This proposal explicitly states the development of decentralized slice indexing by Project Cayley and the operational subsidy for Blockfrost’s free-tier infrastructure. Furthermore, the “Prior Treasury Receipts” section discloses the history of past Treasury receipts. Regarding Article 2, Section 7, Paragraph 3, the requested amount for this proposal is ₳7,920,000, which falls within the 350M ADA Net Change Limit applicable to Epochs 613-713 at the time of submission. Furthermore, the requested amount is stated in ADA, and the USD equivalent is provided for reference only. Article 2, Section 7, Paragraphs 4 and 5 describe the audit and oversight framework, including third-party guarantees, the Oversight Committee, audits, reporting obligations, and dashboards, and designate Intersect as the administrator. Furthermore, Article 2, Section 7, Paragraph 6 describes the use of Treasury Reserve Smart Contract (TRSC) and Project-Specific Smart Contracts (PSSC) for segregated management, a structure designed for on-chain auditability, non-delegation to SPOs, and delegation to auto-abstain DReps. We determine that this structure is intended to align with the requirements of “auditable by the Cardano Community,” “separate accounts,” “not delegated to a SPO,” and “delegated to a predefined abstain voting option.” Therefore, as no clear conflict with the Cardano Constitution has been identified, we determine that this proposal is constitutional.
body.counterargumentDiscussion: This project employs a fund management structure in which funds are temporarily pooled into a TRSC and subsequently distributed to individual projects via PSSC, utilizing the same receiving address as other projects. This structure raises interpretive issues regarding the requirements for “segregated accounts” and “community auditability” set forth in the Cardano Blockchain Ecosystem Constitution. First, regarding the “segregated accounts” requirement, this structure is designed such that funds from multiple projects are aggregated into a single TRSC at the time of receipt, which does not align with address-level segregation in the strict sense. On the other hand, considering the purpose-specific fund management via the PSSC and control through smart contracts, it is possible to interpret that logical segregation has been achieved. Next, regarding “auditability by the community,” tracking is reportedly possible via on-chain data and dashboards, and it can be assessed that auditability is technically ensured. However, since this auditability relies on a certain level of understanding, there remains room for consideration as to whether it meets a standard that is intuitive and easily verifiable. Overall, the proposal was deemed constitutional; however, one of our members abstained, arguing that certain interpretive issues exist in relation to Article 2, Section 7, Paragraph 6 of the Constitution, making it difficult to reach a clear determination of constitutionality.
body.conclusion: For the reasons stated above, we determine that it is constitutional.
